title: Quiz: Optimization Benchmarks and Metrics
description: Test your understanding of circuit quality metrics and benchmarking techniques

questions.question: Why is T-count an important metric for fault-tolerant quantum computing?

options.text: T gates are the fastest gates to execute
T gates require expensive magic state distillation in fault-tolerant schemes
T gates are the only non-Clifford gates
T-count determines the circuit depth

questions.explanation: In fault-tolerant quantum computing with surface codes, T gates cannot be implemented transversally and require magic state distillation, which is resource-intensive. Minimizing T-count directly reduces the overhead of fault-tolerant execution.

questions.question: In a weighted cost model, why are two-qubit gates typically assigned higher costs than single-qubit gates?

options.text: They are harder to implement in software
They have higher error rates on real quantum hardware
They take longer to compile
They require more classical control signals

questions.explanation: Two-qubit gates (like CNOT) have significantly higher error rates on real quantum hardware compared to single-qubit gates, often 10x or more. The weighted cost model reflects this by assigning higher costs to operations that are more likely to introduce errors.

questions.question: What is circuit depth?

options.text: The total number of gates in the circuit
The number of qubits used
The number of time steps needed when parallelizing gates
The number of two-qubit gates

questions.explanation: Circuit depth is the minimum number of time steps (or layers) needed to execute the circuit when gates on different qubits are parallelized. It measures the critical path length and relates to decoherence exposure time.

questions.question: Reducing gate count always reduces circuit depth.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: Gate count and depth are related but distinct metrics. Removing gates might not change depth if the remaining gates still form the same critical path. Conversely, some optimizations might increase parallelism (reduce depth) without changing gate count.

questions.question: Why should optimization benchmarks include diverse circuit types (GHZ, QFT, random, etc.)?

options.text: To make the benchmark take longer
Different optimizers may excel on different circuit structures
It is required by quantum computing standards
To test all possible quantum algorithms

questions.explanation: Different optimization techniques may be more or less effective depending on circuit structure. For example, a rotation-merging optimizer might excel on QFT but provide little benefit for GHZ. Diverse benchmarks reveal the strengths and weaknesses of each approach.

questions.question: Which metric is most relevant for NISQ (Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum) devices?

options.text: T-count only
Qubit count only
CNOT count and circuit depth
Single-qubit gate count

questions.explanation: On NISQ devices, two-qubit gates (like CNOT) have the highest error rates, and circuit depth determines decoherence exposure. Minimizing both CNOT count and depth is crucial for achieving reliable results on current noisy hardware.
